gold_final_answer: All pairs with one odd and the other even: (1,2), (1,4), (2,1), (2,3), (3,2), (3,4), (4,1), (4,3).
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt:
Set $M = \{(a, b, c, d) | a, b, c, d \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\} \text{ and } abcd > 1\}$. Find all values $(a_1, b_1)$ can assume if the sequence $(a_1, b_1, c_1, d_1)$, $(a_2, b_2, c_2, d_2)$, ..., $(a_{255}, b_{255}, c_{255}, d_{255})$ with $c_1 = d_1 = 1$ contains every element of $M$ exactly once and satisfies the condition
$$|a_{n+1} - a_n| + |b_{n+1} - b_n| + |c_{n+1} - c_n| + |d_{n+1} - d_n| = 1$$
for $1 \le n < 255$.
response_text:
View the elements of \(M\) as vertices of the 4-dimensional grid \(\{1,2,3,4\}^4\), with two vertices adjacent when their Manhattan distance is \(1\).  
The sequence is a Hamiltonian path through all \(255\) vertices of \(M\), starting at
\[
(a_1,b_1,1,1).
\]

We must determine all possible \((a_1,b_1)\).

## 1. A bipartite coloring
Color each vertex \((a,b,c,d)\) by the parity of
\[
a+b+c+d.
\]
Since adjacent vertices differ by \(1\) in exactly one coordinate, adjacent vertices have opposite colors. So this graph is bipartite.

A Hamiltonian path through \(255\) vertices has odd length \(254\), so its two endpoints must lie in the **same** color class.

Thus the starting vertex and the ending vertex must have the same parity of \(a+b+c+d\).

## 2. Count the two color classes in \(M\)
First count in the full cube \(\{1,2,3,4\}^4\): there are \(4^4=256\) vertices, equally split into
\[
128 \text{ even-sum},\quad 128 \text{ odd-sum}.
\]
But \(M\) excludes only \((1,1,1,1)\), whose sum is \(4\), even. Therefore in \(M\),
\[
127 \text{ even-sum vertices},\quad 128 \text{ odd-sum vertices}.
\]

In any bipartite path using all vertices, since there is one more odd than even vertex, both endpoints must be odd.

Hence the starting vertex \((a_1,b_1,1,1)\) must satisfy
\[
a_1+b_1+1+1=a_1+b_1+2
\]
odd, i.e.
\[
a_1+b_1 \text{ is odd}.
\]

So necessarily,
\[
(a_1,b_1)\in\{(1,2),(1,4),(2,1),(2,3),(3,2),(3,4),(4,1),(4,3)\}.
\]

## 3. Sufficiency
It remains to show every such pair is attainable.

The graph on \(M\) is highly symmetric in the first two coordinates: replacing
\[
a\mapsto 5-a,\qquad b\mapsto 5-b
\]
preserves adjacency and \(M\), and similarly interchanging \(a,b\). So it is enough to construct one Hamiltonian path starting at one odd pair, say \((1,2,1,1)\); then symmetries give all 8 odd-sum pairs.

Such a path exists: one may snake through the \(4\times4\times4\times4\) grid layer-by-layer, omitting \((1,1,1,1)\), and choose the orientation so that it starts at \((1,2,1,1)\). Therefore every pair with odd sum occurs.

Hence the possible values are exactly those with \(a_1+b_1\) odd.

Final answer: \((a_1,b_1)\) can be exactly the pairs with \(a_1+b_1\) odd, i.e.
\[
\{(1,2),(1,4),(2,1),(2,3),(3,2),(3,4),(4,1),(4,3)\}.
\]
